2. The DeepLab Family — A Brief History

children.label: 2. The DeepLab Family — A Brief History

children.value: is a series of semantic segmentation models developed by Google Research. Each version introduced key innovations:
Introduced atrous (dilated) convolutions to preserve spatial resolution
Introduced Atrous Spatial Pyramid Pooling (ASPP) for multi-scale context
Improved ASPP with batch normalization and image-level global context
Added an encoder-decoder structure for sharper object boundaries
, the most advanced version of the series.
) is a modified convolution operation where gaps are inserted between the kernel elements.
A standard convolution with a 3×3 kernel sees a 3×3 region of the input. An atrous convolution with the same 3×3 kernel but a
sees a 5×5 region — effectively expanding the receptive field without adding extra parameters or reducing spatial resolution.
Why does this matter for segmentation?

children.label: Why does this matter for segmentation?

children.value: to progressively downsample feature maps, which increases the receptive field but loses spatial detail. For segmentation, we need both:
to understand global context (what object is this?)
to accurately locate object boundaries (where exactly does it end?)
Atrous convolution achieves both simultaneously by expanding the receptive field
controls the spacing between kernel elements:
→ one zero inserted between each kernel element

Visualization of atrous convolution with increasing dilation rates.
is a module first introduced in DeepLabV2 and significantly improved in DeepLabV3. It addresses a fundamental challenge in segmentation:
in real-world images — a car nearby looks very different from a car far away.
, each with a different dilation rate, to the same feature map. This creates several parallel “views” of the input at different scales:
The outputs from all parallel branches are concatenated and fused together, giving the model a rich, multi-scale representation.

By looking at the same feature map through multiple “lenses” simultaneously, ASPP lets the model correctly segment both large objects (e.g., a building) and small objects (e.g., a traffic sign) in the same image without needing to resize the image or run multiple passes.
DeepLabV3+ extends DeepLabV3 by adding a dedicated
module on top of the encoder, resulting in a full
. This allows the model to recover fine spatial details that are lost during encoding.
Overview of the DeepLabV3+ encoder-decoder architecture.
is responsible for extracting rich semantic features from the input image. In DeepLabV3+, the encoder has two main components:

as its backbone — deep convolutional networks pre-trained on ImageNet. The backbone is modified to use atrous convolutions in its later layers, allowing it to maintain higher spatial resolution than the standard backbone would.
The ASPP module (described in Section 4) sits at the end of the backbone and produces a multi-scale feature representation. Its output captures broad contextual information about the scene.
from ASPP (rich in semantic information, lower resolution)
from an earlier layer of the backbone (less semantic, but higher resolution — useful for boundary refinement)
takes the encoder’s outputs and progressively refines the segmentation mask to recover sharp object boundaries.
The decoding process works as follows:
The high-level ASPP feature map is
The low-level feature map from the encoder backbone is passed through a
to reduce its channel depth (reducing noise while keeping spatial detail).
along the channel dimension, combining semantic richness with spatial precision.
are applied to refine the combined features.
This simple but effective decoder significantly improves the sharpness of predicted object boundaries compared to DeepLabV3, which used only bilinear upsampling at the output.
A central efficiency innovation in DeepLabV3+ is
(from MobileNet / Xception) factorizes a standard convolution into two steps:
— applies a single filter per input channel (spatial filtering)
— combines the outputs across channels (channel mixing)
compared to standard convolution, with minimal loss in accuracy.
applies the atrous dilation to the depthwise convolution step, so the operation is both computationally efficient
Atrous separable convolution merges the large receptive field of atrous convolution with the efficiency of depthwise separable convolution.
By replacing all standard convolutions in the Xception backbone with atrous separable convolutions, DeepLabV3+ achieves state-of-the-art accuracy while remaining fast enough for practical use.
Losing spatial resolution during feature extraction
Parallel atrous convolutions at different dilation rates
Combines deep semantic features with shallow high-res features
Factorizes convolution for efficiency without accuracy loss
combines all of these ideas into a unified architecture that achieves excellent performance on standard benchmarks (PASCAL VOC, Cityscapes) while being practical to train and deploy.
